utterances: What better way to celebrate post-Valentine's Day weekend than with a movie about betrayal?
And we're talking real betrayal, government secrets betrayal.
In wide release, Chris Cooper stars in the drama ""Breach,"" based on the real life story of FBI turncoat Robert Hanssen.
Ryan Phillippe and Laura Linney also star.
(Soundbite of movie, ""Breach"")
(As Eric O'Neill) I think he keeps shooting his mouth off about the bureau and nobody knows what to do with him, so we tag him as a deviant and run him out of the building.
The critics applaud ""Breach"" and especially praise Cooper's lead performance.
Newsweek raves, A wonderfully taut cat-and-mouse thriller.
And the Christian Science Monitor shouts, Without Cooper's performance ""Breach"" would've been a good workman-like thriller.
His presence lifts it to a whole new level.
For those who prefer a more traditional Valentine movie, we have the annual Hugh Grant romantic comedy.
This one's called ""Music and Lyrics."" Grant plays a washed-up 1980s pop star who has a shot at a comeback but needs Drew Barrymore's help.
(Soundbite of movie, ""Music and Lyrics"")
(As Alex Fletcher) Rolling Stone called it a crass, contrived effort.
Not even good enough for a dentist's chair.
(As Sophie Fisher) Well, I'm sure there were other reviews.
There were.
There were.
But none as good as that one.
Even thought the majority of the critics did find the movie predictable, many also find it charming.
Unusually witty and intelligent, gushes the Seattle Post-Intelligencer.
The Chicago Tribune says, Grant and Barrymore are very enjoyable together onscreen.
And the Baltimore Sun advises, ""Music and Lyrics"" doesn't pretend to be anything more than a cheerful night out.
And on that count it scores.
And the Newbery Award-winning children's novel, ""Bridge to Terabithia,"" has finally been adapted for the big screen.
The story focuses on two classmates who supposedly discover a magical kingdom deep in the forest.
(Soundbite of movie, ""Bridge to Terabithia"")
(As Jesse Aarons) Leslie, get behind me.
(As Leslie Burke) Turn around.
There's more.
Many of the nation's critics loved crossing this bridge.
ReelViews cheers, Easily the best family feature of the early year.
The Los Angeles Times finds it a wonderfully heart-wrenching love story for tweens, teens and even adults.
And the Washington Post says, The beloved children's book has not just survived but thrived in its adaptation to the screen.
Finally, it wouldn't be a movie weekend without a film based on a comic book about a flaming skull that rides a flaming motorcycle hunting down demons.
Yes, it's ""Ghost Rider,"" with Nicolas Cage starring as stunt jumping biker Johnny Blaze, along with Eva Mendes as his true love and - oh yeah, Peter Fonda as the devil himself.
(Soundbite of movie, ""Ghost Rider"")
(As Roxanne Simpson) You know you can talk to me.
You can tell me anything.
(As Johnny Blaze) You wouldn't believe anyway.
(As Simpson) Try me.
(As Blaze) I sold my soul to the devil.
(As Simpson) Mm-hmm.
The reviews are just coming in and so far so not so good.
The Montreal Film Journal shrugs, If you're expecting no more than B movie fun, it does deliver on that level.
But Reel Film hates it, snarling, Undoubtedly one of the worst comic book adaptations to hit theaters.
And the New York Post howls, ""Ghost Rider"" is heading off a cliff and Cage is steering.
I just don't understand how the Ghost Rider isn't pulled over by the cops all the time.
Is there a problem, officer?
Yeah.
I'm arresting you for DWOF - driving while on fire.
And geez, those tires are burning up pretty good.
(Soundbite of cough)
I'm also going to see your smog check certificate.
Do you have any idea how much CO2 gases are emitted from a flaming skull?
Mark Jordan Legan is a writer living and breathing easy in Los Angeles.
The Supreme Court begins its new term next week.
The headline cases involve abortion, affirmative action, and global warming.
Here to give us a preview is Dahlia Lithwick.
She's legal analyst for the online magazine Slate and for us here at DAY TO DAY.
Hi, Dahlia.
Hey, Madeleine.
Well, let's dive into those big cases.
First up, abortion.
Tell us about the cases the justices have agreed to hear.
Is that a common Indonesian name?
No.
My dad, he was doing business in the prawn industries.
And I was named (laughter) - I was named after the industry.
That was an expedient, to basically say to the Americans, look, I'm not a war-phobic president.
I'm not insensitive to our security needs.
Those resources should've been focused on Afghanistan where, Obama argued, our genuine national security interests lie with respect to the war on terror on al-Qaida.
When Obama first moved into the White House, the rhetoric didn't change much.
In February, he announced that all U.S.
combat troops would be withdrawn from Iraq within two years.
Then a month later, he delivered a major speech detailing a new strategy for Afghanistan and Pakistan, the country it shares a mountainous border with.
He called the region the most dangerous place in the world.
The situation is increasingly perilous.
It's been more than seven years since the Taliban was removed from power, yet war rages on, and insurgents control parts of Afghanistan and Pakistan.
The headline from that March speech was that the president would send an additional 21,000 troops to Afghanistan.
Part of the goal was to provide a secure environment for the Afghan elections in August.
It all seemed a continuation of the policy laid out during the campaign: more resources, more troops.
But also in that speech was a call for a complete review of the mission in Afghanistan — which is where we are today.
And now, events of the past month have further complicated things.
A leaked report by the top U.S.
commander in Afghanistan, General Stanley McChrystal, says many more troops are needed or the U.S.
risks failure.
At the same time, American casualties in Afghanistan in July and August were the highest since the war began.
The election did take place, but there are charges of widespread corruption, and there's still no declared winner.
And suddenly, the White House direction on Afghanistan is far less clear.
Here's Press Secretary Robert Gibbs.
This isn't going to be finished in one meeting.
It's not going to be finished in several meetings.
But this is the beginning of a process for making some eventual determinations.

It is worth noting that two of Mr. Obama's presidential rivals from last year are now in the room as he considers his options: Hillary Clinton is secretary of state and there's Vice President Joe Biden, who has long favored focusing the U.S.
mission in Afghanistan more narrowly on counterterrorism with fewer U.S.
troops.
That approach contains risks.
Rejecting a request from the Pentagon could affect the president's relationship with the military and prompt his critics to revive attacks that he's weak on national security.
But polls also show the public growing weary of the Afghanistan war.
This past Friday, Mr. Obama said he expects tough questions.
We're not going to arrive at perfect answers.
I think anybody who's looked at the situation recognizes that it's difficult and it's complicated.
But my solemn obligation is to make sure that I get the best answers possible, particularly before I make decisions about sending additional troops into the theater.
The administration resists any notion that what the president said during the campaign puts him in a box.
The White House insists that the only goal is to find a strategy that works.
Don Gonyea, NPR News, Washington.
We're back with ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News.
I'm Guy Raz.
Over night, the country relegated one part of its broadcast spectrum to the history books.
The switch to digital television is complete.
So, say goodbye to rabbit ears, wavy lines on your screen, and to static.
(Soundbite of static)
Few of you listening, I suspect, will mourn the passing of static.
But there is one guy who's devastated.
That static has been music to the ears and to the eyes of artist Rick Doble.
For years, he's been photographing television static to create works of art.
This weekend, digital television robbed him of his palette.
Mr.
Doble joins us now on the line from his home in North Carolina.
Mr. Doble, hello.
